speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 18th of February, 1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, when you left Dzambul to come back to Poland, was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left her in Dzambul.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left her-- in Dzambul, we lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, did you leave her there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went together in one cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, did you go back to the Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. We passed Lutsk, Kivertsi-- the station, Kivertsi, passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The junction, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the junction. And they, the Ukraine and the Poles start to shoot on us, because they know that this is a Jewish cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you didn't-- and-- and you stopped there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were-- we stopped for a little while, and we moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nobody got off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody got off. That's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So this time you were now back with your sister and her husband and the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there? From Kiwerce?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- we went to Poland, near the-- near the German border in a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the-- to kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five months. I'm not sure 100%.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you reunited with anybody from Lutsk? Did you see any old faces?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see old faces from Lutsk, but I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of your school friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody. Did you find anybody who knew what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know to this day what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- she was killed. They took all the Jewish people to the-- from the town, far from the town. They dig themself a big hole and they shoot each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I show you the photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me what happened here.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- one lady pretends that she-- she was killed with her child. And the night she went out--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she fell into the pit.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to the pit. And she went out, and she run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you meet up with her afterward?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not meet her there at all. She wrote a letter to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she told you what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: She told use what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was amongst those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't say who was. She just say what happened to the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And did she-- where was she living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she ride from, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't got idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your letter came, and told you what happened to the people of Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there-- were there any of your family or your husband's family that survived besides your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's family, only an auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: For when we've been in Poland already. He went to Warsaw. He's from Warsaw. And he went to the cemetery. And he went-- he has uncle, he has a big family-- is well known in Warsaw, the family Brodaty. His uncle, when he died, the Jewish community want 3 million zlotys to bury him. And they didn't want to give it. But they beg him. Eventually, he-- they get a million, or whatever. And he went to his grave, and he put a letter-- whoever survived in Warsaw, he came from-- oh, I can't-- not Bratslav. I can't remember the name of the-- Chechen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chechen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chechen-- whoever survived to I-- I miss Chechen in kibbutz. And fair enough, auntie came. And the men who was there, looking after the grave. Fine-- auntie came, my husband's auntie came, to-- to the grave. And he said, you have a letter. She faint.

speaker: interviewer
text: She fainted?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- [CRYING] and then find us. And she came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was-- yes, no she came to Germany. And there we'd been living together in one home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then she went to Israel. And we went here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kibbutzed over to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you were living on the kibbutz in Poland, what were you doing there for the months that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been working in the kindergarten as a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry, what was he doing? I mean, your husband, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband went to clean the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And he worked for the municipality?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for municipality. We hadn't been long there. From there, the-- the Jewish community-- the Jewish organization organized us to go back to-- to go to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do they send you to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany, when we could go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't go from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Some people-- later on, they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: But initially, you had to go to Germany. Which town did you go to, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a funny part when we'd cross the border, we couldn't take anything with us. And my-- we got $10. We sold some things in Poland, and we have $10.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how can he take it? So my husband took it, put it in a box with cigarettes. They said you can take 10 packets of cigarettes in one. And he opened it nicely and put it there, $10. Into a pack of cigarettes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- he put it there. $10 in a match of boxes. And they took away this pack of cigarettes, at the border-- the German. And they took the match, and my husband take a cigarette. Just walk and take the box of matches, put in the pocket. That's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: A miracle, because he had to ask them for a match to light the cigarette. That was clever.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very clever. My husband was a very clever man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from the kibbutz to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think. I don't remember. I'm sure that it was the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came to Munich. Where did you live when you came to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kirchseeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a suburb of Munich, or is it a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A suburb. This was a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave you a place to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us a place to live and a German family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived with a German family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a room. A separate there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they know. They used to say, and Henry was born there-- do you understand German?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] He was a big boy. Fattish boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they say, can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They liked him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain it? Can you explain what you said in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they eat the-- the-- how do you say, the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: The boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy. Not the [NON-ENGLISH]. It's not a boy. The seat-- the seat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, so they were very fond of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very fond of him. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in that little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I couldn't get to hospital. All of a sudden, in  the  night, I wake up, and the water run out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who delivered him?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a midwife. I'd been walking and a sheet between my  legs. [LAUGHS] And when walking to the midwife. And then she delivered. I delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was healthy and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was healthy. I have my health.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you got to Munich was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very--my husband used to go every day, and I stay with the boy, with the little child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Munich. He was going--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirchseeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kirchseeon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I been living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how long after you got there was Henry born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh we'd been living-- oh, a year or more. Because we left-- we came here, Henry was 14 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And from Kirchseeon you left. You came here, to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: To French, to France, to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Paris. And who did you go to in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hyatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How that took-- In

speaker: interviewee
text: A hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we met-- my husband had a put in England in London. They had a cousin. He came to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew that you'd survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We'd been in constant contact with him. And my sister from America used to send me every month, parcels. And for that, we'd been eating in Germany, in Kirchseeon. The German people like it. There were the clothes-- every-- every few weeks I had a parcel from Poland-- from America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your other sister? Was she with you in Kirchseeon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She went-- she went from Chechen-- she went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went to Kirchseeon. No, she went from Chechen to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So she didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait a minute. She didn't go to Israel. She went to Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. In Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Salzburg. Not Austria-- she went somewhere in Germany, in another town. And from there, she went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why didn't you go to Israel, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband said-- to go-- to the golden land we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was his plan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If we haven't got any protection.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you stay in Kirchseeon all that time? Were you waiting for papers? Or why did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because auntie was there. My husband's auntie was there. And we'd been living there. And my husband used to go to-- to Munich every day. He took a course of-- what? To make soap-- to learn how to make soap, and some other thing. I don't remember. And I was sitting at home in Kirchseeon with a little baby, by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your auntie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auntie used to go, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: She used to get out to work or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to work-- she went-- she went Munich every day. What she did, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, how-- what-- when did you leave Kirchseeon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went from Kirchseeon to Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks. And then from Paris we went by plane to Belgium. And from Belgium here. A plane who made-- a army plane. Was about 70 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody on the plane that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were people that had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. Hyatt, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to in Israel? Where did you go? When did you come here to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away to Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which day? Do you remember when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September. What day, I don't remember. I know that Mr. Bowen-- Bowman pick us up, the man who sent us the permit. And put us in King's Cross in a posh hotel. With a little baby, he said, don't go out. People doesn't-- the Australian people doesn't like foreigners. Doesn't want to talk. We're sitting, not eating all the day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And even if you came to visit us, we told him, we hungry. Oh, he said, my God. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep touch with Mr. Bowman.

speaker: interviewee
text: From time to time. From the hotel, we went to-- he find for us a home in the basement in Balmoral. And there-- no water, no toilet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we find a friend, Mr. Cherytok, and he find for us a place in King's Cross. One room divided-- half was kitchen, half was the bedroom. No water and no toilet and not. It was a [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a-- a gathering.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we start to work, gradually. Did you sew, when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband. Oh, that was a disaster. He couldn't-- a nail he couldn't build. He was  such a-- but I went to a factory-- a knitting factory, Shirley. And because I'm very good with my hand. And so they took him and learn. He taught him how to knitting, too-- the machine-- it was the hand knit. He could-- he comes in the night, he couldn't move his hand, because was hand machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you both worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both, we worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry wasn't at school yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry was in kindergarten. When we been living in North Shore, we couldn't find a kindergarten for him. Wasn't kindergartens at all. And we find the Knoll- I don't know if you know-- the Knoll, they have a pharmacy factory. They find a German woman who took him in for to stay there with them, and I went to work. And once a week, Sunday, we came there to-- to visit him. They been sitting in the lunch  room eating, and Henry was sitting outside eating by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have other children, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I been, a few times. I had abortions. I couldn't afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're actually finished with your story now. I just want to know what impressions remain with you from the war years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a very unhappy period of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the worst thing for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger. Starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: For all this, all the years of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we had, sometimes not. Was a time we didn't have salt in the village. And I can't eat-- I vomit, straight away when I eat without salt. That's why I eat a lot of salt now. I have to make my own bread. Actually, not bread-- they're called-- pancakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No fire. We have to keep fire all the time. We have to cover it in the morning. We have to blow it to make fire. No matches.

speaker: interviewer
text: No water. What can I tell you? Was absolutely disaster. Do you have-- that people will see this movie that you have made, and it will be used for educational purposes, and archival purposes. Do you have a message for the-- for people who see your movie? See your film, for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Live while you can. Every day is a winning day. Hope that's one to repeat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Sarah, we're going to have a look at your photographs now. And you'll just explain who the people are on the photographs that we see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is my father. His name was Elkana.

speaker: interviewer
text: His surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gets. Actually, his real name was Gelman, but in the olden ages his father didn't want him to go to the Russian army, so they change it for Getz, as a German name. Because he was one-- they didn't take if it was a single man. A single child.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother. She was killed when the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Chana Leah. And that's all what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my mother, and my sister and brother. My sisters and brother. And this was taken before, I think it's '35, '34 or '35. My sister left in '35 to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sisters and one brother. That was taken in 1980--

speaker: interviewee
text: '68. And this was the wedding of my niece.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was it taken, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel. And you came from all over the world. You got together in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older sister with her two children and husband. And when this is taking and where? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken before the war, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The children were small before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you traveled with them, when you left Lutsk then, the children were much smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their names, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elkana and Shula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their surnames? You remember their sur--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dreisin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dreisin. And they survived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They survived with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sister and her husband and me, of course. And this was taken in Lutsk near the river Styr.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1935, My auntie from America came to visit us. And this was taken in the Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recognize anybody in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my auntie, my mother, my auntie, another auntie, my sister, and my brother in law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your auntie in America's name, the one who came to visit? Your sister came to visit from America.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she's not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's not there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see her. That's not her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the photo was taken on the occasion of her visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- I don't remember now, because I thought it is her. But it is not her. Auntie Brocha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband. And it's me and his best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. We meet actually in Russia. And we went together to Poland to Chechen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live Chechen? You went together with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went together with him. We lived in the in house. And then we went to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your husband know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They finished together high school in Warsaw, he knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does he live now? [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died. When he arrived in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband's family. And here's my husband as a child. His brother and father and mother. The father died in 1935. And the others were killed in the wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from the grave where the survivor were kill-- where the people from Lutsk were killed. And after the war they make a photo of this. And this is some people who came from around the world to-- for-- to the grave. And here's my cousin who died in Kanada.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son. That's was 11 months old. And the photo was taken in Germany where he was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my husband, Jacob, and my son, Henry, and me. Taken, I think here-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guess it must be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's older. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my son, Henry Brodaty, and my husband. His graduation from university. He finished medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me, my husband, my daughter in law, my granddaughter, Nina--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband isn't on the photo. Yes, he's in the photo. No, that's Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry-- ah, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son. I'm sorry. I keep forgetting. And that was David's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my certificate of marriage. Approximate in Munich, before we left for Australia. That's me, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it was to certify that they had been married, and who they were. For that was important for them coming to a new country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera three.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Henry, which we came together, as a child. And that's my little grandson, David, which I adore. And-- and I have a granddaughter. She went to Israel. And my daughter in law couldn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your granddaughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter in law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Caroline.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very pleased that my mother's made this video, because it's important for us to have a record of what happened. And so we can see what you've been through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Louder.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's always been telling me what to do. And as you can see in this video, nothing's changed. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you stop it? Please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure what you talked about, but I know something about the experiences that you and [NON-ENGLISH] went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah. OK. And David, you want

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: David?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know much about what happened to [NON-ENGLISH], but I don't know. I think it's really important that she does this for not only me to know what happened, but other people, just in general. And I'm just happy that she's done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape three of the interview with Sarah Brodaty. It's the 18th of February, 1996. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
